required.title: [Reading Material] A rich, diverse library of books at and slightly above the student's reading level
[Read Aloud — by the Mentor] Chapter books and longer works
[Script Introduction] Age-appropriate materials for one additional script

suggested.title: [Reading Log] A simple notebook or form
[Nonfiction] DK Eyewitness books, National Geographic Readers, or similar

supplementary.title: [Script Resource] YouTube videos showing how to write in Cyrillic, Hangul, or other scripts

competence_markers.description: The student reads grade-level text with accuracy and appropriate pace — decoding is becoming automatic rather than effortful
The student comprehends what they read and can narrate stories and information with detail and sequence
The student reads independently for at least 20 minutes daily and has an established reading habit
The student self-selects books and can articulate why they chose them
The student can distinguish between narrative and informational text and adjusts reading attention accordingly
The student's vocabulary is growing through reading — they notice and collect unfamiliar words
The student can decode basic characters in one script other than Latin (Cyrillic, Hangul, or Greek)
The student reads aloud with expression, demonstrating comprehension through intonation and pacing
The student shows genuine engagement with books — reading is a valued part of their daily life, not merely an assignment

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me what happened in your book today.
narration_prompts.detail: Start from where you started reading and tell me the story up to where you stopped. (This is the daily narration — keep it brief and natural.)

narration_prompts.prompt: Who is the most interesting character in your book, and why?
narration_prompts.detail: What do they want? What is getting in their way?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me something you learned from a nonfiction book this week.
narration_prompts.detail: What was the most surprising fact? What do you want to know more about?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between reading a story and reading a book about real things?
narration_prompts.detail: How do you read them differently?

narration_prompts.prompt: Read me your favorite passage from a book you love.
narration_prompts.detail: Then tell me why you chose it.

narration_prompts.prompt: Show me what you learned about your new writing system.
narration_prompts.detail: Write some characters for me. Sound out a word. What is the hardest part? What makes sense?

cross_connections.description: The student who reads widely writes better — vocabulary, sentence patterns, and narrative structures are absorbed through reading and reproduced in writing. Reading and writing are the two halves of literacy, and they develop best together.

As the student reads more complex text, they encounter more complex sentence structures — subordinate clauses, compound sentences, varied word order. The grammatical awareness from 3.1.1 helps the student parse these structures.

The second script introduction connects directly to 3.1.4's exploration of different sound systems. The student who is learning Cyrillic or Hangul is seeing how a different culture encoded its sounds into visual form.
The student who can now read independently can access books about history, geography, and civilization on their own. Reading becomes a tool for learning across every domain.
The student who can decode a second script is building the foundation for reading functional signs and labels in other languages — a practical skill that 3.1.3 will develop further.

The daily independent reading habit is a discipline practice as much as a literacy practice. The student who reads every day because it is their habit — even on days when they do not feel like it — is building the self-governance that IC1.1 develops.

content.why_this_matters:
You can read. You cracked the code in A1 — you know how letters represent sounds, you can sound out words, and you can read simple books. That was the hardest part. What comes next is what makes reading powerful.

Right now, reading probably still feels like work. You see a word and you have to sound it out, think about it, then move on to the next word. By the time you finish a sentence, you may have forgotten how it started. This is normal. Your brain is still building the pathways that make decoding automatic — so fast that you do not even notice you are doing it, and all your attention can go to meaning.

That shift — from effortful decoding to automatic, fluent reading — is what this packet develops. When reading becomes fluent, everything changes. You can read for information, for pleasure, for discovery. You can read a chapter book and be so absorbed in the story that you forget you are reading at all. You can pick up a book about animals, or space, or history, or how machines work, and teach yourself things no one has told you. A fluent reader is a person who can learn anything.

This packet also deepens your awareness of writing systems beyond English. While you are becoming a stronger reader of the Latin alphabet, you will begin to learn your way around a second script — actually reading a few characters and words in a system that is not English. This is the beginning of the script literacy that will grow throughout your education.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Reading fluency** — the ability to read with accuracy, appropriate speed, and expression, freeing attention for comprehension
2. **Comprehension skills** — understanding what a text says, making inferences, connecting information, and retelling with detail
3. **Independent reading habit** — the daily practice of reading self-selected books for sustained periods, which is the engine of all subsequent reading growth
4. **Expanding vocabulary through reading** — learning new words from context, the primary mechanism of vocabulary growth from this age forward
5. **Basic text awareness** — understanding that different texts are organized differently (stories have characters and plots; nonfiction has topics and facts)
6. **Introduction to a second script** — beginning to decode characters in one writing system beyond the Latin alphabet
content.core_content:
### Part 1: From Decoding to Fluency — What Changes

In A1, you learned to decode — to look at letters and translate them into sounds and words. Decoding is like learning to walk. At first, every step requires concentration. You wobble. You fall. You have to think about each movement.

Fluency is like running. Your legs know what to do. You do not think about how to take a step — you think about where you are going. A fluent reader does not think about how to read a word — they think about what the word means and what happens next in the story.

**How fluency develops:** Through reading. Not through drills, not through exercises, not through speed tests. Through reading — a lot of reading, every day, of books that are interesting and at the right level of difficulty.

The research is clear: the single most important factor in developing reading fluency is volume of reading. The student who reads 20 minutes a day encounters approximately 1.8 million words per year. The student who reads 5 minutes a day encounters approximately 282,000 words. The student who reads 1 minute a day encounters approximately 8,000 words. The differences in exposure are enormous, and they compound over time. By the end of elementary school, the avid reader has encountered millions more words than the reluctant reader — and that gap in experience produces a gap in fluency, vocabulary, background knowledge, and comprehension that widens every year.

This is sometimes called the "Matthew Effect" in reading: the rich get richer and the poor get poorer. The student who reads well reads more, which makes them read better, which makes them read more. The student who reads poorly avoids reading, which means they get less practice, which keeps them reading poorly. The single most important thing this packet does is establish the daily reading habit that puts the student on the right side of this cycle.

### Part 2: Reading for Meaning — Comprehension

Fluency without comprehension is just saying words out loud. Real reading means understanding what you read — knowing what happened, why it happened, what the characters feel, what the author means, and what you think about it.

**What good comprehension looks like at this age:**

- **Retelling:** The student can tell you what happened in a chapter or story, in order, with detail. Not "it was about a dog" but "there was a dog named Winn-Dixie who showed up at a grocery store, and a girl named Opal decided to keep him, and..."
- **Asking questions:** The student wonders about what they read: "Why did the character do that?" "What will happen next?" "Is this really true?" Questions are a sign of active engagement.
- **Making connections:** The student connects what they read to other things they know: "This is like the story we read about ancient Egypt" or "This reminds me of when we went to the river."
- **Inferring:** The student understands things the text implies but does not state directly. If a character slams a door and stomps upstairs, the student understands the character is angry, even though the word "angry" was never used.
- **Noticing vocabulary:** The student encounters an unfamiliar word and either figures it out from context or asks about it. Their vocabulary is growing through reading.

**How to build comprehension:** Talk about what you read. After reading a chapter, tell someone about it. After finishing a book, describe it to a friend or family member. Narration — retelling in your own words — is the practice that builds comprehension. The student who reads but never talks about what they read retains far less than the student who reads and narrates.

### Part 3: Choosing Books — Finding What You Love

One of the most important skills you will develop in this packet is the ability to choose books for yourself. Not every book assigned by someone else — books you want to read because the subject interests you or because someone whose opinion you trust recommended it or because you read the first page and could not stop.

**How to find good books:**

- **Browse.** Go to the library. Walk through the shelves. Pick up books that catch your eye. Read the first page. If it grabs you, take it home. If it does not, put it back. There is no obligation to finish every book you start (though you should give a book at least 20–30 pages before deciding).
- **Follow your interests.** If you love animals, read about animals — fiction and nonfiction. If you love mythology, read every mythology book you can find. If you love funny books, read funny books. There is no hierarchy of "good" reading material at this age. The student who devours books about sharks is building fluency just as effectively as the student who devours books about princesses.
- **Ask for recommendations.** Your mentor, a librarian, a friend who reads a lot — people who know books can point you to titles you would never have found on your own.
- **Read series.** When you find a series you love — Magic Tree House, Junie B. Jones, Diary of a Wimpy Kid, Wings of Fire, whatever it is — read all of them. Series reading is one of the most effective paths to fluency because the familiar characters and style reduce cognitive load, letting the student process text faster and build stamina.

### Part 4: Different Kinds of Text

Not everything you read works the same way. Understanding the difference helps you read better.

**Stories (narrative text)** have characters, a setting, a problem, and a resolution. When you read a story, you follow what happens. You pay attention to who the characters are, what they want, what gets in their way, and how things turn out.

**Informational text (nonfiction)** is organized around topics, not plots. A book about the solar system does not have characters or a storyline — it has facts, explanations, and pictures organized by subject. When you read nonfiction, you are reading to learn something. You pay attention to the main idea, the details that support it, and any new vocabulary.

**Poetry** uses language differently from stories or nonfiction. Poems pay attention to how words sound — their rhythm, their rhyme, their repetition. Reading poetry aloud is often the best way to experience it, because the sound is part of the meaning.

You will read all three kinds during this packet. Each requires a slightly different kind of attention, and practicing all three makes you a stronger, more flexible reader.

### Part 5: Building Your Reading Life

The most important thing about reading is doing it. Every day. Not because someone makes you, but because you have discovered that reading is one of the best things a person can do.

**Daily independent reading:** Set aside at least 20 minutes every day for reading. This is your time to read whatever you choose, uninterrupted. Twenty minutes is a floor, not a ceiling — if you want to read for an hour, read for an hour. The habit is what matters: every day, you read.

**The reading log:** Keep a simple record of what you read. The title, the pages, and one thought about it. This is not a book report. It is a record of your reading life — and looking back at the list of books you have read is one of the most satisfying things a reader can do.

**Reading aloud:** Continue reading aloud sometimes, even though you can read silently. Reading aloud builds expression, rhythm, and awareness of how sentences sound. Read aloud to a younger sibling, a parent, or even a pet. The audience does not matter. The practice does.

### Part 6: Your Second Script — Beginning to Read Beyond English

You know that writing systems differ — you explored this in A1. Now you are going to take the next step: actually learning to decode some characters in a script that is not the Latin alphabet.

Your mentor will help you choose a script based on your context and interests. Three strong options:

**Cyrillic** (used for Russian, Ukrainian, Bulgarian, Serbian, and others): Cyrillic is an alphabet, like English — each letter represents a sound. Many Cyrillic letters look like Latin letters but represent different sounds (for example, "P" in Cyrillic represents the /r/ sound, not /p/). Learning Cyrillic is manageable because it works on the same principle as the English alphabet — you are just learning new shapes for sounds.

**Korean Hangul** (used for Korean): Hangul was designed to be easy to learn. The consonant shapes represent the position of the tongue in the mouth. The vowels are built from simple lines. Letters are grouped into syllable blocks. Hangul is one of the most logical writing systems ever created.

**Greek** (used for Greek, and familiar from mathematics and science): Many Greek letters are already familiar — alpha, beta, delta, pi. Learning the Greek alphabet connects to mathematics (3.5.1) and to the history of ancient Greece (1.1.2). It is also relatively small and systematic.

**What you will do:** Learn the letters of your chosen script. Practice writing them. Sound out simple words — even without knowing what they mean. Read your name in the new script. The goal is not to read a language. The goal is to prove to yourself that a different writing system is learnable — that the shapes that look mysterious today can become familiar with practice.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: Daily Independent Reading [Ongoing — Backbone of the Packet]
Read for at least 20 minutes every day from self-selected books. This is the single most important activity in the packet. The mentor ensures the student has access to books, a quiet time for reading, and no competing distractions during reading time. After reading, the student narrates what they read — a brief retelling to the mentor or in the reading log.

### Study 2: Read-Aloud Continuation [Ongoing]
The mentor reads aloud to the student at least four days per week from a chapter book or other rich text. After each reading session, the student narrates what they heard. The read-aloud text should be more complex than what the student can read independently — this continues to build vocabulary, background knowledge, and comprehension beyond the student's current reading level.

### Study 3: Narration After Reading [Ongoing]
After every independent reading session and every read-aloud, the student narrates — tells back what happened, what they learned, or what they noticed. Narrations may be oral or, as the student's writing develops, written. The mentor listens for detail, sequence, and personal engagement. This is the primary comprehension practice and the primary assessment.

### Study 4: Genre Exploration [Early-to-Mid Packet]
Deliberately guide the student through different types of text: a fiction chapter book, a nonfiction book on a topic of interest, and a collection of poetry. After each, discuss: How was this different from the other kinds of books? What did you have to pay attention to? Which did you enjoy most? The student discovers their preferences — and also discovers that different reading material requires different kinds of attention.

### Study 5: Vocabulary Collection [Ongoing]
The student keeps a running list of new words encountered in reading. For each word, they write the word, the sentence they found it in, and what they think it means (from context). The mentor discusses the words periodically. This is not a vocabulary quiz — it is training the habit of noticing and collecting unfamiliar words.

### Study 6: Second Script Introduction [Mid-to-Late Packet]
Over four to six sessions, the student learns the basic characters of their chosen second script. They practice writing the characters. They sound out simple words. They write their name in the new script. By the end, the student can look at a short text in the new script and sound out at least some of the words — even without knowing the language. This is the proof of concept: scripts are learnable.

### Study 7: Reading Aloud with Expression [Late Packet]
The student selects a passage from a favorite book and practices reading it aloud with expression — changing voice for different characters, pausing at commas and periods, reading dialogue the way the character would say it. This is not performance for its own sake. Reading with expression requires comprehension (you have to understand the text to know how it should sound) and reinforces fluency.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Fluency
**Developing well:** The student reads at a comfortable pace without frequent pausing to decode. They read with natural expression — their voice goes up for questions, pauses at commas, drops for periods. When they encounter an unfamiliar word, they attempt to decode it and usually succeed. Reading sounds like talking, not like a robot.

**Developing slowly:** Reading is still labored and word-by-word. The student pauses frequently to sound out common words. There is little expression — the reading is monotone. The student avoids reading because it is still effortful.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student reading text at the right level? If more than 5% of the words require effortful decoding, the text is too hard for fluency building. Drop to easier text where the student can read with high accuracy (95% or better). Fluency develops from reading easy text fluently, not from struggling through hard text.

### 2. Comprehension
**Developing well:** The student can retell what they read with detail and sequence. They make inferences — understanding things the text implies but does not state. They ask questions about what they read. They connect what they read to things they already know. They notice when something does not make sense.

**Developing slowly:** Narrations are brief and vague. The student can say what a book is "about" but cannot retell specific events or details. They do not ask questions about their reading. They cannot explain why a character did something or what the main idea of a nonfiction passage is.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student reading material that is interesting to them? Comprehension is dramatically affected by engagement. A student who is bored by the assigned book will comprehend less than a student who chose the book themselves. Also: are you modeling comprehension in your read-alouds? When you read aloud and then talk about the book — wondering, questioning, connecting — you are showing the student what active comprehension looks like.

### 3. Reading Volume and Habit
**Developing well:** The student reads daily without being reminded. They carry books with them. They talk about what they are reading. They have opinions about books. They ask for more books.

**Developing slowly:** The student reads only when required. They do not choose to read during free time. They have no strong preferences about books.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the book supply adequate? Is reading time genuinely protected, or does it get displaced by screens, activities, and errands? Does the student have books that match their actual interests? Have you tried audiobooks for the student who resists print but loves stories? (Audiobooks build vocabulary and comprehension even if they do not build decoding fluency — they can be a bridge to print.)

### 4. Vocabulary Growth
**Developing well:** The student uses words in narration and conversation that they learned from reading. They notice unfamiliar words and either figure them out from context or ask about them. Their spoken vocabulary is expanding visibly.

**Developing slowly:** The student skips unfamiliar words without noticing. Their spoken vocabulary is not growing despite reading.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling vocabulary awareness? When you encounter an interesting word in a read-aloud, pause and discuss it. "Listen to that word — 'magnificent.' What do you think it means? Where have you heard it before?" This teaches the student to notice words.

### 5. Second Script Progress
**Developing well:** The student can identify and write the letters of their chosen second script. They can sound out simple words in the script. They show curiosity about the script and enjoy the novelty of decoding something that looks different.

**Developing slowly:** The student shows no interest in the second script or forgets previously learned characters between sessions.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the introduction engaging, or has it become another drill? Keep the script work playful at this age — writing their name, decoding fun words, finding the script on real-world objects (Russian labels, Greek letters in math books, Korean on restaurant menus). If it feels like school, it will fail.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Testing Instead of Reading
The moment you start giving comprehension quizzes, you have changed reading from a pleasure into a performance. The student begins reading with anxiety about what they will be asked rather than with attention to what the text says. Narration — telling you about what they read in their own words — assesses comprehension without the destructive side effects of testing.

### Restricting Book Choice
"That book is too easy." "That book is junk." "You should be reading something more challenging." These statements kill reading motivation. The student who rereads a favorite book for the fifth time is building fluency through comfortable, enjoyable reading. The student who reads "easy" books is reading — which is the point. As fluency grows, the student will naturally reach for more complex material. Do not force the pace.

### Abandoning the Read-Aloud Too Soon
Many parents and mentors stop reading aloud once the child can read independently. This is a mistake. The read-aloud serves purposes that independent reading cannot yet serve: exposure to complex text, modeling of fluent reading, shared literary experience, and the pure pleasure of being read to. Continue reading aloud at least through age 10, ideally longer.

### Confusing Slow Reading with Poor Reading
Some students read slowly but comprehend deeply. They process every sentence carefully and think about what they read. This is not a fluency problem — it is a reading style. If the student's narrations are detailed and engaged, and the student enjoys reading, do not push for faster reading. Speed without comprehension is not a goal.
